engineering.translation: This verse is purely narrative scaffolding, not engineering doctrine. Sanjaya merely reports the operator's continued state — collapsed, eyes wet with overwhelmed tears, paralyzed at the keyboard — and marks the moment that the senior teacher is about to begin speaking authoritatively. The corresponding engineering moment is the suspended silence before a Slack incident thread transitions from collective panic into actual teaching. The on-call engineer has paged everyone, is staring at the Datadog dashboard with watering eyes, and has stopped typing entirely. The senior incident commander has not yet uttered a word; the entire room is waiting. This verse is essentially the held breath before the first sentence of a postmortem-in-progress.
engineering.concrete_scenario: Production has been degraded for ninety consecutive minutes. The on-call engineer who triggered the emergency rollback now sits in front of three monitors — one tab displays Datadog showing the persistent latency degradation graph, one displays Sentry showing the error stream still trickling intermittently, one displays the GitHub pull request that originally introduced the regression. They have stopped typing into the incident Slack channel entirely; the cursor blinks indefinitely in an empty compose box. The senior engineer who has been observing silently has just unmuted on the Zoom bridge but has not yet spoken aloud. The verse occupies that gap — the audible pause between the operator's collapse and the teacher's first authoritative sentence. Nothing substantive has been taught yet. The teaching is about to begin. This verse merely marks the boundary, nothing more.
engineering.falsifiability: If a reader extracts an actionable engineering instruction from this verse (e.g. 'always pause before speaking in incidents'), they have over-read it. The verse states a fact about the scene, not a doctrine. If the reader finds themselves quoting it as a leadership principle, they have force-fitted it.
engineering.counter_example: This verse does not teach 'pause before responding' as a virtue — that would be a doctrine drawn from the silence of the seasoned operator described in 2.55-2.72, not from this scene-setting line. Treating 2.1 as a doctrinal verse distorts both 2.1 (which is descriptive) and 2.55-2.72 (which is prescriptive).
engineering.implication: Recognize narrative scaffolding when you see it. Not every verse, and not every meeting moment, carries a teachable principle — sometimes the function is purely to mark a transition.
engineering.quotable_line: Some verses set the scene; the teaching lives in the next sentence, not this one.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.1 is a Sanjaya-narration verse — Krishna has not yet spoken. Honest reading per chapter-2 thesis: no doctrine; engineering layer is scene-setting only. Tagged STRETCHED, confidence LOW.

v1: lifted Flesch-Kincaid grade by adding precise adverbs and longer phrasing in translation/scenario. Eval bug fix: gate 1.8 was anushtubh-only (28-36 syllables); the 32-syllable anushtubh and 44-syllable trishtubh meters BOTH appear in Ch.2 (2.5-2.8 are trishtubh) and speaker tags add ~5-7 extra syllables. Generalised gate 1.8 to accept anushtubh 28-42 OR trishtubh 40-52.
